content.text:
<analysis>
a. Summarize the event type and context:
   - This is a PULL_REQUEST event with an open PR #151 titled "feat: Review PSEPK phaA GO annotations"
   - The PR is on branch feat/review-psepk-phaa -> main
   - It's an AI-assisted review of P. putida KT2440 gene phaA GO annotations as part of the P_PUTIDA project
   - The PR adds several files including the gene review YAML, research notes, and cached publications

b. Determine if this is a request for code review feedback or for implementation:
   - This is explicitly a request for code review feedback based on the direct_prompt asking me to "review this pull request and provide feedback on code quality, best practices, potential bugs, security concerns, test coverage"

c. List key information from the provided data:
   - 10 changed files with 7834 additions and 6509 deletions
   - Main addition is genes/PSEPK/phaA/phaA-ai-review.yaml (256 lines)
   - Supporting files include deep research, notes, UniProt data, GOA data, and cached publications
   - Large change to cache/go/terms.csv (GO terms cache update)

d. Outline the main tasks and potential challenges:
   - Review the gene review YAML file for compliance with schema and best practices
   - Check the quality of the annotation reviews and curation decisions
   - Validate that references are properly cited and accessible
   - Ensure the review follows the project guidelines from CLAUDE.md
   - Check for any potential issues with the GO term cache update

e. Propose a high-level plan of action:
   - Read the CLAUDE.md file to understand project guidelines
   - Examine the main phaA-ai-review.yaml file for structure and content quality
   - Review the supporting research and notes files
   - Check citation formatting and reference accessibility
   - Validate against schema if possible using the validation commands mentioned in CLAUDE.md
   - Provide comprehensive feedback on the gene review quality and adherence to guidelines

f. No obvious permission issues expected for this review task as it's primarily reading and analyzing files.
</analysis>

input.body: ### PR Review: PSEPK phaA GO Annotations I'll review this pull request focusing on code quality, best practices, and adherence to project guidelines. #### Todo List: - [ ] Read project guidelines from CLAUDE.md - [ ] Review main gene review YAML file structure and content - [ ] Examine supporting research and notes files - [ ] Check citation formatting and reference accessibility - [ ] Validate schema compliance - [ ] Provide comprehensive feedback summary [View job run](https://github.com/ai4curation/ai-gene-review/actions/runs/23372185422)

content.content:
1→# AI instructions for ai-gene-review
     2→
     3→## Project Overview
     4→
     5→This project is for managing AI reviews of gene annotations. The overall goal is to both
     6→review existing GO annotations using strict defined criteria, and to summarize the resulting
     7→high quality annotations.
     8→
     9→## Repository layout
    10→
    11→src/
    12→  ai_gene_review/
    13→    schema/
    14→      gene_review.yaml <-- linkml schema for review docs
    15→genes/
    16→  human/
    17→    CFAP300/
    18→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- keep your notes here
    19→      CFAP300-ai-review.yaml <-- the output of your AI review
    20→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- your own notes, edit this, use it like a journal and keep appending.
    21→      CFAP300-uniprot.txt <-- the uniprot record downloaded from the UniProt website (do not edit this file)
    22→      CFAP300-goa.tsv <-- the GO annotation downloaded from the QuickGO API (do not edit this file, no need to view it)
    23→      CFAP300-deep-research.md <-- the deep research for the gene review (do not edit this file, it is automatically generated for you)
    24→      CFAP300-citations.md <-- the citations for the gene review (do not edit this file)
    25→      CFAP300-bioinformatics/ <-- the folder for any bioinformatics analyses (create this if necessary)
    26→      CFAP300-predictions-review.yaml <-- review of computational predictions NOT in GOA (optional)
    27→    <other genes>/
    28→      ...
    29→  worm/
    30→    lrx-1/
    31→  SCHPO/  <-- other orgs use uniprot species codes
    32→      ...
    33→publications/
    34→  PMID_123456.md <-- cached publication text (this should be available for you, DO NOT EDIT)
    35→reactome/
    36→  R-HSA-NNNNNN.md <- cached reactome entry
    37→
    38→You can regenerate the derived files by running commands like:
    39→
    40→```
    41→just fetch-gene human CFAP300 
    42→```
    43→
    44→This makes the uniprot, goa, and publications files. It will also create a stub for the ai-review.yaml if it doesn't exist.
    45→
    46→After making edits to the ai-review.yaml file, you can validate the file by running:
    47→
    48→```
    49→just validate human CFAP300
    50→```
    51→
    52→This ensures that the yaml file is consistent with the schema in `src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml`, and also
    53→that it implements best practices.
    54→
    55→To validate all files, run:
    56→
    57→```
    58→just validate-all
    59→```
    60→
    61→files will be written to reports/validation-all.tsv 
    62→
    63→* All gene symbols follow the standard nomenclature for that species. For human, should be HGNC symbols.
    64→
    65→## Gene Review Guidelines
    66→
    67→Your job is to review existing annotations for a gene, following GO guidelines, and synthesize these with what is known in the literature,
    68→and what can be inferred bioinformatically. The general structure of the review is as follows:
    69→
    70→```yaml
    71→id: <uniprot id>
    72→gene_symbol: <HGNC symbol>
    73→taxon:
    74→  id: <NCBI taxon id>
    75→  label: <taxon label>
    76→description: <your own summary of the gene>
    77→references:
    78→  <all relevant reference, including both literature and bioinformatics sources; includes all but not limited to those used in existing_annotations>
    79→existing_annotations:
    80→  <review of existing annotations, using the GO guidelines; should include all annotations from goa.csv>
    81→core_functions:
    82→  <your own summary of the core functions of the gene>
    83→proposed_new_terms:
    84→  <suggestions for new GO terms>
    85→suggested_questions:
    86→  <suggested questions for experts>
    87→suggested_experiments:
    88→  <suggested experiments to elucidate gene function>
    89→```
    90→
    91→See the schema and existing files for more details.
    92→
    93→When creating a new review, always make sure files are in place:
    94→
    95→`just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`
    96→
    97→This creates the uniprot, goa files, and a stub for the gene review.
    98→
    99→The next thing is for you to do deep research on that gene and make a GENE-notes.md file, with citations inline (e.g [PMID:12345 title, "<supporting text>"]). In some cases this will have been done ahead of time for you.
   100→
   101→## Reviewing existing annotations
   102→
   103→You should start with the ai-review.yaml file, where stubs are created for you (from `just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`). Any PMID cited should be in the publications folder, e.g. `publications/PMID_123456.md`. Review these.
   104→
   105→A deep research file should already be present in the gene folder, e.g. `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-deep-research-PROVIDER.md` (where PROVIDER is openai, falcon, perplexity, etc.). This should contain a summary of the literature, and any other information you found useful for the review. There may be irrelevant information in the citations, that is OK. If this is not present, you can generate one using:
   106→
   107→```bash
   108→just deep-research SPECIES GENE --provider perplexity
   109→```
   110→
   111→This will create a file like `GENE-deep-research-perplexity.md`. You can also make your own notes in `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-notes.md`.
   112→
   113→Be aware that many GO terms are over-annotations. You should also not take existing annotations as gospel, whether experimental or bioinformatic.
   114→always use a holistic synthesized picture of the gene function that you have obtained from your research.
   115→
   116→```yaml
   117→ActionEnum:
   118→    permissible_values:
   119→      ACCEPT:
   120→        description: Accept the existing annotation as-is, no modifications, and retain as representing the core function of the gene
   121→      KEEP_AS_NON_CORE:
   122→        description: Keep the existing annotation as-is, but mark it as non-core. For pleiotropic genes, this may be the developmental
   123→          processes, or other processes that are not the core function of the gene.
   124→      REMOVE:
   125→        description: Remove the existing annotation, as it is unlikely to be correct based on combined evidence
   126→      MODIFY:
   127→        description: The essence of the annotation is sound, but there are better terms to use (use in combination with proposed_replacement_terms).
   128→          if the term is too general, then MODIFY should be used, with a proposed replacement term for the correct specific function.
   129→          sometimes terms can also be overly specific and contorted, so in some cases you might want to generalize
   130→      MARK_AS_OVER_ANNOTATED:
   131→        description: The term is not entirely wrong, but likely represents an over-annotation of the gene
   132→      UNDECIDED:
   133→        description: The annotation is not clear, and the reviewer is not sure what to do with it. ALWAYS USE THIS IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO ACCESS
   134→          RELEVANT PUBLICATIONS
   135→      NEW:
   136→        
   137→```      
   138→
   139→## Tools
   140→
   141→Use the OLS MCP to find relevant ontology terms, if the terms you need are not in existing_annotations.
   142→
   143→## Curation guidelines
   144→
   145→Avoid the term `protein binding`, this doesn't tell us anything about the actual function. Instead find a more
   146→informative MF term (e.g for adapter function)
   147→
   148→## Isoform and Negation Tracking
   149→
   150→The system tracks isoform-specific GO annotations and NOT (negated) annotations:
   151→
   152→```yaml
   153→existing_annotations:
   154→# Isoform-specific annotation
   155→- term:
   156→    id: GO:0045892
   157→    label: negative regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   158→  evidence_type: IDA
   159→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   160→  isoform: P19544-1  # UniProt isoform ID - only present for isoform-specific annotations
   161→  review:
   162→    summary: ...
   163→
   164→# NOT annotation (negated)
   165→- term:
   166→    id: GO:0045893
   167→    label: positive regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   168→  evidence_type: IDA
   169→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   170→  negated: true  # This is a NOT annotation
   171→  isoform: P19544-1
   172→  review:
   173→    summary: WT1 does NOT activate transcription...
   174→```
   175→
   176→- **`isoform`**: UniProt isoform ID (e.g., `P19544-1`) - indicates what isoform was tested
   177→- **`negated`**: Set to `true` for NOT annotations (e.g., `NOT|involved_in` qualifier in GOA)
   178→
   179→**Important**: Just because an annotation was made on a specific isoform doesn't mean the function is isoform-specific.
   180→The `isoform` field indicates what was tested, not necessarily what is unique to that isoform.
   181→
   182→To backfill isoform info on existing reviews:
   183→```bash
   184→just backfill-isoforms human WT1
   185→just backfill-isoforms-organism human  # all genes in organism
   186→```
   187→
   188→See `docs/isoform_tracking.md` for full documentation and `projects/ISOFORMS.md` for genes with notable isoform-specific functions.
   189→
   190→## Bioinformatics analyses
   191→
   192→In some cases, it may be useful to do additional bioinformatics analyses. To validate gene function. Here are some guidelines:
   193→
   194→For a gene-specific analysis, the folder is `<genes/specues/GENE>-bioinformatics/`. You should make this be reproducible. You can do a `uv init`
   195→here and `uv add` for any packages you need.
   196→
   197→Never hardcode the results of the script, it's OK for a script to return inconclusive results. Never ever make up results.
   198→
   199→**IMPORTANT**: DO NOT write scripts that just tell users to go to a website. If you cannot programmatically call a tool (e.g., requires API keys, browser interaction, or authentication you don't have), then:
   200→1. Be honest about this limitation upfront
   201→2. Write scripts that can parse/process the output from the tool if the user runs it manually
   202→3. Never write misleading code that pretends to do something it doesn't actually do
   203→
   204→You can summarize the results of the bioinformatics analyses in the `<GENE>-bioinformatics/RESULTS.md` file. This can be referenced as any other reference
   205→as e.g `file:<SPECIES>/<GENE>/bioinformatics/RESULTS.md`.
   206→
   207→## Reviewing Computational Predictions
   208→
   209→The `GENE-predictions-review.yaml` file reviews computational/ML predictions that are NOT
   210→in the curated GOA/UniProt annotations. This is separate from `existing_annotations` in the
   211→main review file.
   212→
   213→Use the `PredictionReview` class (validated with `-C PredictionReview`):
   214→
   215→```bash
   216→uv run linkml-validate -s src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml -C PredictionReview genes/ECOLI/yciO/yciO-predictions-review.yaml
   217→```
   218→
   219→### Prediction sources
   220→
   221→Track predictions from: DeepECTF, PANTHER/IBA, InterPro2GO, CLEAN, GloEC, MAPred, or any
   222→other computational method that produces GO or EC predictions.
   223→
   224→### Assessment categories (from de Crécy-Lagard et al. 2025, PMID:40703034)
   225→
   226→| Code | CS | Description |
   227→|------|-----|-------------|
   228→| COR  | 2   | Correct novel prediction |
   229→| CNN  | 2   | Correct but Not Novel (already in training data) |
   230→| LSP  | 2   | Less Precise than existing annotation |
   231→| UNC  | 1   | Uncertain - cannot validate or refute |
   232→| PLI  | 0   | Paralog Incorrect - wrong paralog subfamily |
   233→| NPI  | 0   | Nonparalog Incorrect - refuted by evidence |
   234→| REP  | 0   | Repetition - frequency-biased prediction |
   235→
   236→### Error types
   237→
   238→- `PARALOG_OVERANNOTATION` - Wrong paralog subfamily (Type 6)
   239→- `PATHWAY_CONTEXT_IGNORED` - Predicted pathway absent from organism
   240→- `FREQUENCY_BIAS` - Model defaults to high-frequency training labels
   241→- `IN_VITRO_NOT_IN_VIVO` - In vitro activity doesn't match biological function
   242→- `TRAINING_DATA_CONTAMINATION` - Prediction already in training data
   243→- See schema for full list
   244→
   245→## General guidelines
   246→
   247→* NEVER guess identifiers for terms, genes, publications. Always use the relevant tools or MCPS, or look them up in derived files.
   248→* For files `<GENE>-notes.md`, use literature deep search, and always record provenance for assertions, e.g `[PMID:12345 "<supporting text>"]`
   249→
   250→## Support code
   251→
   252→There is also support code in `src/ai_gene_review`, see the CLAUDE.md file in that directory for more details
   253→on best practices for working with the code.
   254→
   255→
   256→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer high-level questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

input.body: ### PR Review: PSEPK phaA GO Annotations I'll review this pull request focusing on code quality, best practices, and adherence to project guidelines. #### Todo List: - [x] Read project guidelines from CLAUDE.md - [ ] Review main gene review YAML file structure and content - [ ] Examine supporting research and notes files - [ ] Check citation formatting and reference accessibility - [ ] Validate schema compliance - [ ] Provide comprehensive feedback summary [View job run](https://github.com/ai4curation/ai-gene-review/actions/runs/23372185422)

content.content:
1→id: Q88D25
     2→gene_symbol: phaA
     3→product_type: PROTEIN
     4→status: DRAFT
     5→taxon:
     6→  id: NCBITaxon:160488
     7→  label: Pseudomonas putida (strain ATCC 47054 / DSM 6125 / CFBP 8728 / NCIMB 11950
     8→    / KT2440)
     9→description: >-
    10→  Poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) polymerase 1 (PhaC1), a Class II PHA synthase that catalyzes
    11→  polymerization of (R)-3-hydroxyalkanoyl-CoA thioesters into medium-chain-length
    12→  polyhydroxyalkanoates (mcl-PHAs, C6-C14). PhaC1 is the primary PHA synthase in P. putida
    13→  KT2440, encoded as the first gene in the phaC1-phaZ-phaC2-phaD operon (PP_5003). PHAs
    14→  accumulate as intracellular granules serving as carbon and energy storage reserves under
    15→  nutrient-limiting conditions. The gene is designated phaA in the KT2440 genome annotation
    16→  but referred to as phaC1 in the broader PHA literature.
    17→existing_annotations:
    18→- term:
    19→    id: GO:0016746
    20→    label: acyltransferase activity
    21→  evidence_type: IEA
    22→  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000002
    23→  review:
    24→    summary: >-
    25→      PhaC1 (PP_5003) is annotated with EC 2.3.1.- (acyltransferase) based on its genome
    26→      annotation (PMID:12534463). The enzyme catalyzes a transesterification reaction,
    27→      transferring acyl groups from (R)-3-hydroxyalkanoyl-CoA donors to a growing polyester
    28→      chain. GO:0016746 (acyltransferase activity) is a correct but relatively general
    29→      molecular function term. The slightly more specific child term GO:0016747
    30→      (acyltransferase activity, transferring groups other than amino-acyl groups) would be
    31→      more informative, since PHA synthases transfer hydroxyalkanoyl groups, not amino-acyl
    32→      groups. However, no PHA-synthase-specific MF term exists in GO, so GO:0016746 is
    33→      acceptable as a broad classification. The InterPro match to IPR011287 (PHA_synth_II)
    34→      is correct for this Class II PHA synthase.
    35→    action: ACCEPT
    36→    reason: >-
    37→      The acyltransferase activity annotation is correct. PhaC1 is classified as EC 2.3.1.-
    38→      and catalyzes acyl group transfer from CoA-linked substrates to polyester chains.
    39→      While GO:0016747 would be slightly more specific, GO:0016746 is not wrong and
    40→      accurately captures the molecular function. The InterPro domain match (IPR011287,
    41→      PHA_synth_II) is a high-confidence match for this Class II PHA synthase.
    42→    supported_by:
    43→      - reference_id: PMID:12534463
    44→        supporting_text: >-
    45→          points to potential new applications in agriculture, biocatalysis,
    46→          bioremediation and bioplastic production
    47→      - reference_id: PMID:17137299
    48→        supporting_text: >-
    49→          The biosynthesis of mcl-PHA was enhanced only by the overexpressed phaC1 gene
    50→- term:
    51→    id: GO:0042619
    52→    label: poly-hydroxybutyrate biosynthetic process
    53→  evidence_type: IEA
    54→  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000002
    55→  review:
    56→    summary: >-
    57→      GO:0042619 (poly-hydroxybutyrate biosynthetic process) refers specifically to PHB, a
    58→      short-chain-length polymer of beta-hydroxybutyrate (C4). However, P. putida KT2440
    59→      PhaC1 is a Class II PHA synthase that preferentially polymerizes medium-chain-length
    60→      (mcl, C6-C14) 3-hydroxyalkanoyl-CoA substrates, producing mcl-PHAs -- NOT
    61→      poly-hydroxybutyrate (PHB). PHB biosynthesis is the function of Class I PHA synthases
    62→      such as PhaC from Cupriavidus necator. This annotation is an incorrect assignment from
    63→      the InterPro2GO mapping: both IPR010941 (PhaC_N) and IPR011287 (PHA_synth_II) map to
    64→      GO:0042619, but the InterPro entries cover all PHA synthases (Class I and II) while the
    65→      GO term is specific to PHB. There is no broader GO term for polyhydroxyalkanoate
    66→      biosynthetic process that would correctly describe the mcl-PHA biosynthesis carried out
    67→      by this enzyme.
    68→    action: REMOVE
    69→    reason: >-
    70→      This annotation is factually incorrect for P. putida PhaC1. The enzyme is a Class II
    71→      PHA synthase that produces medium-chain-length PHAs (mcl-PHAs, C6-C14), not
    72→      poly-hydroxybutyrate (PHB, C4). The GO term GO:0042619 explicitly describes PHB
    73→      biosynthesis, defined as a polymer of beta-hydroxybutyrate. The InterPro2GO mapping
    74→      that generates this annotation does not distinguish between Class I (PHB-producing) and
    75→      Class II (mcl-PHA-producing) synthases. P. putida KT2440 does not produce PHB. No
    76→      appropriate broader GO BP term for polyhydroxyalkanoate biosynthesis currently exists;
    77→      this represents a gap in the GO ontology.
    78→    additional_reference_ids:
    79→      - PMID:17137299
    80→      - PMID:20406286
    81→    supported_by:
    82→      - reference_id: PMID:17137299
    83→        supporting_text: >-
    84→          The biosynthesis of mcl-PHA was enhanced only by the overexpressed phaC1 gene
    85→- term:
    86→    id: GO:0070088
    87→    label: polyhydroxyalkanoate granule
    88→  evidence_type: ISS
    89→  original_reference_id: PMID:17137299
    90→  review:
    91→    summary: >-
    92→      PHA synthases are integral components of PHA granules. The GO term GO:0070088
    93→      (polyhydroxyalkanoate granule) description explicitly states that PHA synthase is one
    94→      of the proteins associated with PHA granules. PhaC1 catalyzes the polymerization
    95→      reaction at the granule surface and is physically associated with PHA granules in vivo.
    96→      This is a well-established localization for Class II PHA synthases in Pseudomonas
    97→      species.
    98→    action: NEW
    99→    reason: >-
   100→      PHA synthase is explicitly listed in the GO:0070088 definition as a constituent
   101→      protein of PHA granules. PhaC1 is the primary PHA synthase in P. putida KT2440 and
   102→      localizes to PHA granules where it catalyzes polymer chain elongation. Direct
   103→      experimental evidence from Kelly et al. (2024) used PhaC1-eYFP fusion as a positive
   104→      control for carbonosome localization in fluorescence microscopy. This cellular
   105→      component annotation is missing from the current GOA set and should be added.
   106→    supported_by:
   107→      - reference_id: PMID:38608840
   108→        supporting_text: >-
   109→          eYPF fusions to PhaI and PhaC1 served as positive controls since both are reported
   110→          to localize to the carbonosome
   111→      - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaA/phaA-deep-research-falcon.md
   112→        supporting_text: >-
   113→          PHA granules (carbonosomes) are intracellular hydrophobic inclusions; proteins
   114→          involved in polymerization and regulation are associated with granule surfaces
   115→- term:
   116→    id: GO:0016747
   117→    label: acyltransferase activity, transferring groups other than amino-acyl groups
   118→  evidence_type: ISS
   119→  original_reference_id: PMID:17137299
   120→  review:
   121→    summary: >-
   122→      GO:0016747 is a more specific child of GO:0016746 (acyltransferase activity) that
   123→      excludes amino-acyl transfer. PHA synthases transfer hydroxyalkanoyl groups from CoA
   124→      to a growing polyester chain, which falls under this more specific term. This would
   125→      provide a more informative molecular function annotation than the current GO:0016746.
   126→    action: NEW
   127→    reason: >-
   128→      PhaC1 transfers (R)-3-hydroxyalkanoyl groups from CoA-linked substrates to a growing
   129→      polyester chain. This is a non-amino-acyl acyltransfer reaction, making GO:0016747 a
   130→      correct and more specific alternative to the existing GO:0016746 annotation. Adding
   131→      this more specific term improves annotation precision.
   132→    supported_by:
   133→      - reference_id: PMID:17137299
   134→        supporting_text: >-
   135→          The biosynthesis of mcl-PHA was enhanced only by the overexpressed phaC1 gene
   136→references:
   137→- id: GO_REF:0000002
   138→  title: Gene Ontology annotation through association of InterPro records with GO terms
   139→  findings: []
   140→- id: PMID:12534463
   141→  title: Complete genome sequence and comparative analysis of the metabolically versatile
   142→    Pseudomonas putida KT2440
   143→  findings:
   144→    - statement: >-
   145→        The P. putida KT2440 genome encodes PP_5003 (phaA/phaC1) as a poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate)
   146→        polymerase with EC 2.3.1.- classification.
   147→      supporting_text: >-
   148→        points to potential new applications in agriculture, biocatalysis,
   149→        bioremediation and bioplastic production
   150→- id: PMID:26913973
   151→  title: The revisited genome of Pseudomonas putida KT2440 enlightens its value as a
   152→    robust metabolic chassis
   153→  findings: []
   154→- id: PMID:17137299
   155→  title: Metabolic engineering and characterization of phaC1 and phaC2 genes from Pseudomonas
   156→    putida KCTC1639 for overproduction of medium-chain-length polyhydroxyalkanoate.
   157→  findings:
   158→    - statement: >-
   159→        PhaC1 is the primary PHA synthase; overexpression of phaC1 enhanced mcl-PHA
   160→        biosynthesis up to 2.86-fold, while phaC2 overexpression did not activate
   161→        mcl-PHA biosynthesis.
   162→      supporting_text: >-
   163→        The biosynthesis of mcl-PHA was enhanced only by the overexpressed phaC1 gene
   164→- id: PMID:20406286
   165→  title: The PhaD regulator controls the simultaneous expression of the pha genes involved
   166→    in polyhydroxyalkanoate metabolism and turnover in Pseudomonas putida KT2442.
   167→  findings:
   168→    - statement: >-
   169→        The phaC1ZC2D operon is regulated by PhaD, a carbon source-dependent transcriptional
   170→        activator of the pha cluster.
   171→      supporting_text: >-
   172→        Mutagenesis analyses demonstrated that PhaD, a TetR-like transcriptional regulator,
   173→        behaves as a carbon source-dependent activator of the pha cluster
   174→- id: PMID:38608840
   175→  title: Comprehensive proteomics analysis of polyhydroxyalkanoate (PHA) biology in
   176→    Pseudomonas putida KT2440 - the outer membrane lipoprotein OprL is a newly identified
   177→    phasin.
   178→  findings:
   179→    - statement: >-
   180→        PhaC1-eYFP fusion was used as a positive control for carbonosome localization,
   181→        confirming PhaC1 localizes to PHA granules in P. putida KT2440 via fluorescence
   182→        microscopy.
   183→      supporting_text: >-
   184→        eYPF fusions to PhaI and PhaC1 served as positive controls since both are reported
   185→        to localize to the carbonosome
   186→    - statement: >-
   187→        PhaC1 APMS identified nine potential interacting proteins including five putative
   188→        lipoproteins; PhaI, PhaC1 and PhaZ are all located on the PHA granule.
   189→      supporting_text: >-
   190→        PhaI, PhaC, and PhaZ are all located on the PHA granule and involved in PHA
   191→        metabolism
   192→- id: file:PSEPK/phaA/phaA-deep-research-falcon.md
   193→  title: Deep research report on phaA/phaC1 (PP_5003) in P. putida KT2440
   194→  findings:
   195→    - statement: >-
   196→        PhaC1 catalyzes polymerization of (R)-3-hydroxyacyl-CoA monomers into mcl-PHA,
   197→        functioning as the key chain-elongating enzyme. Carbon reaches PhaC1 through
   198→        beta-oxidation of fatty acids and de novo fatty-acid synthesis routes.
   199→      supporting_text: >-
   200→        PhaC1 is a PHA polymerase/synthase that polymerizes (R)-3-hydroxyacyl-CoA monomers
   201→        into mcl-PHA
   202→core_functions:
   203→- description: >-
   204→    Catalyzes polymerization of (R)-3-hydroxyalkanoyl-CoA thioesters (preferentially
   205→    medium-chain-length, C6-C14) into polyhydroxyalkanoates (mcl-PHAs) with
   206→    concomitant release of CoA. Functions as the primary PHA synthase in P. putida
   207→    KT2440.
   208→  molecular_function:
   209→    id: GO:0016746
   210→    label: acyltransferase activity
   211→  locations:
   212→    - id: GO:0070088
   213→      label: polyhydroxyalkanoate granule
   214→  supported_by:
   215→    - reference_id: PMID:17137299
   216→      supporting_text: >-
   217→        The biosynthesis of mcl-PHA was enhanced only by the overexpressed phaC1 gene
   218→proposed_new_terms:
   219→  - proposed_name: polyhydroxyalkanoate biosynthetic process
   220→    proposed_definition: >-
   221→      The chemical reactions and pathways resulting in the formation of polyhydroxyalkanoates
   222→      (PHAs), polyesters of hydroxyalkanoic acids that serve as carbon and energy storage
   223→      materials in prokaryotic cells. This includes both short-chain-length PHAs (such as
   224→      poly-hydroxybutyrate) and medium-chain-length PHAs.
   225→    justification: >-
   226→      GO currently has GO:0042619 (poly-hydroxybutyrate biosynthetic process), which is
   227→      specific to PHB (short-chain, C4). There is no broader term covering the biosynthesis
   228→      of all polyhydroxyalkanoates, including medium-chain-length PHAs (mcl-PHAs, C6-C14)
   229→      produced by Class II PHA synthases such as P. putida PhaC1. The cellular component
   230→      term GO:0070088 (polyhydroxyalkanoate granule) uses the broader polyhydroxyalkanoate
   231→      terminology, and a corresponding biosynthetic process term is needed. GO:0042619
   232→      should be a child of this new term.
   233→    proposed_parent:
   234→      id: GO:0044249
   235→      label: cellular biosynthetic process
   236→    supported_by:
   237→      - reference_id: PMID:17137299
   238→        supporting_text: >-
   239→          The biosynthesis of mcl-PHA was enhanced only by the overexpressed phaC1 gene
   240→suggested_questions:
   241→  - question: >-
   242→      Should GO create a broader polyhydroxyalkanoate biosynthetic process term as a parent
   243→      of GO:0042619 (poly-hydroxybutyrate biosynthetic process) to accommodate Class II PHA
   244→      synthases that produce mcl-PHAs?
   245→  - question: >-
   246→      What is the functional distinction between PhaC1 and PhaC2 in P. putida KT2440? Is
   247→      PhaC2 catalytically active under any conditions, or is PhaC1 solely responsible for
   248→      mcl-PHA synthesis?
   249→suggested_experiments:
   250→  - description: >-
   251→      In vitro reconstitution of PhaC1 activity with defined (R)-3-hydroxyalkanoyl-CoA
   252→      substrates of varying chain lengths to establish kinetic parameters and determine
   253→      the minimum and maximum chain length accepted.
   254→  - description: >-
   255→      In vitro enzyme assays with purified PhaC1 using different chain-length
   256→      3-hydroxyalkanoyl-CoA substrates to determine substrate specificity and kinetic parameters.
   257→
